summary: Cantrill argues that the business case for open source should not focus on 'how does this directly help me?' but rather 'how much does it cost me?' and 'does it hurt me?' Using DTrace's inclusion in Mac OS X Leopard as a case study, he demonstrates that open sourcing technology costs essentially nothing (since software has no variable cost), doesn't hurt Sun (since Mac OS X and Solaris aren't direct competitors), and actually helps by expanding the technology's reach in unanticipated ways. The key insight is that open source transforms a product feature into a technological advance that transcends any single company.
key_points: Open source business questions should shift from 'how does this help me?' to 'how much does it cost me?' and 'does it hurt me?'
Software's most beautiful property is that it has no variable cost - only fixed costs matter
The real choice isn't between giving away or selling technology, but between a potential customer using your technology versus someone else's
Open source benefits are often unanticipatable - no one at Sun predicted DTrace would benefit from Ruby/Rails developers on Mac OS X
Technology presence in multiple systems transforms it from a product feature into a technological advance
money_quotes: The thrust of the business questions around open source should not be 'how does this directly help me?' or 'can't I sell them something instead?' but rather 'how much does it cost me?' and 'does it hurt me?'

The choice is not between 'I give a customer my technology' and 'I sell a customer my technology', but rather between 'a customer that I never would have had uses my technology' and 'a customer that I never would have had uses someone else's technology.'
This is possible because of that most beautiful property of software: it has no variable cost; the only meaningful costs associated with software are fixed costs.
It has cost Sun more money in terms of my time to blog how this didn't cost anything to Sun than it did in fact cost Sun in the first place.
The presence of DTrace in other systems assures that it transcends a company or its products – that it moves beyond a mere feature, and becomes a technological advance.
This all serves as an object lesson of how unanticipatable the benefits of open source can be.
key_insight: Open source strategy should be evaluated not by direct revenue impact but by its negligible cost, minimal competitive harm, and potential to transform proprietary technology into industry-standard advances that benefit the originator through broader adoption and ecosystem growth.
